text: A test case is a documented set of conditions, inputs, steps, and expected results used to verify that a specific feature or behavior works as intended. A well-written test case is: independent (does not depend on other tests), repeatable (produces same result every run), clear (another person can execute it without asking questions), and specific (tests exactly one behavior).

Equivalence Partitioning divides input data into groups where all values in a group are expected to behave the same way. Instead of testing every possible input, test one representative value from each partition. Example: age field accepting 18-65. Partitions: below 18 (invalid), 18-65 (valid), above 65 (invalid). Test one value from each partition instead of all possible values.

Boundary Value Analysis tests values at the edges of equivalence partitions where defects are most likely to occur. For a range of 18-65: test 17 (just below), 18 (lower boundary), 19 (just above lower), 64 (just below upper), 65 (upper boundary), 66 (just above). Boundary conditions catch off-by-one errors that equivalence partitioning misses.

Decision Table Testing is used when behavior depends on combinations of conditions. Create a table with all conditions as rows, all possible combinations as columns, and expected actions as the result. Useful for business rules with multiple conditions — insurance eligibility, discount calculations, access control logic. Forces testers to consider all combinations systematically.

State Transition Testing models behavior as states and transitions between them. Useful for systems with clear states: order (pending, confirmed, shipped, delivered, cancelled). Test cases cover: valid transitions (pending to confirmed), invalid transitions (delivered to pending), and boundary states (what happens at each state). Good for workflows, user sessions, and protocol testing.

Test case priority levels help manage limited testing time. Priority 1 (Critical): core business flows that if broken make the system unusable. Priority 2 (High): important features used frequently. Priority 3 (Medium): secondary features with workarounds available. Priority 4 (Low): edge cases and cosmetic issues. In time-constrained releases, always run P1 and P2 before P3 and P4.

A good test case for engineers includes: Test ID, Title (what is being tested), Preconditions (system state before test), Test Steps (numbered, specific actions), Test Data (exact inputs), Expected Result (precise, measurable outcome), Actual Result (filled during execution), and Pass/Fail status. Vague expected results like 'system works correctly' are untestable — expected results must be specific and verifiable.

Negative test cases verify that the system handles invalid input, unexpected behavior, and error conditions correctly. Engineers often only write positive test cases (happy path) — negative testing is where most production bugs hide. For every positive test, ask: what happens with empty input, null values, boundary violations, concurrent access, and network failures?
